title: Find a Peak Element (O(log n))
description:
A peak is an element strictly greater than its neighbors. For an array `nums` (assume `nums[-1] = nums[n] = -∞`), return the index of ANY peak.

Linear scan works in O(n). But there's a clever O(log n) binary-search approach: even when the array isn't sorted, you can always find a peak by repeatedly halving.

**The point**: binary search isn't only for sorted arrays. It works whenever you can rule out half the search space.

examples.explanation: nums[2] = 3 is greater than both neighbors.
Either index 1 or index 5 is a valid peak. Returning either is accepted.

hints: Don't try to sort — that's O(n log n) and not the point.
Pick any index i. Compare nums[i] and nums[i+1]. If we're going 'up', a peak must exist on the right. If 'down', on the left (or at i itself).
Binary search: shrink lo/hi by half each iteration.

testCases.description: single element is always a peak
